Name: perceptual_rate_distortion_compression
Title: Integrating Perceptual Quality into Rate-Distortion Framework for Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: Incorporating perceptual quality measures into the rate-distortion framework will yield more efficient model compressions that maintain task relevance while minimizing perceptual distortion.
Related Work: While existing literature discusses model compression through conventional rate-distortion theory, little attention has been given to integrating perceptual fidelity measures. The proposed work contrasts with existing methods by explicitly considering the perceptual dimensions of the reconstructed models, which can influence user experience and task performance.
Abstract: Model compression techniques often rely on traditional rate-distortion theory to achieve a balance between model size and performance. However, in many applications, especially those involving human interaction, perceptual quality plays a critical role in determining the effectiveness of compressed models. This proposal explores the integration of perceptual metrics into the rate-distortion framework for model compression. By framing perceptual fidelity as an additional constraint in the optimization process, we hypothesize that we can achieve models that not only adhere to theoretical limits of compression but also enhance user experience through improved perceptual quality. We will derive new bounds and optimization strategies that reflect this integrated approach, providing a clearer understanding of the trade-offs between task performance and perceptual fidelity. The outcomes will be validated through computational experiments comparing traditional compression methods against our proposed framework on various datasets.
Experiments: 1. Derive a modified rate-distortion function that incorporates perceptual quality as a constraint. 2. Develop a quantization algorithm that optimizes both traditional distortion and perceptual fidelity. 3. Conduct experiments on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and evaluate models based on traditional metrics (accuracy, compression rate) and perceptual metrics (SSIM, PSNR). 4. Compare the performance of the proposed method against baseline compression techniques using task-relevant distortion measures.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Difficulty in quantifying perceptual quality across different tasks or domains. 2. Potential increase in computational complexity due to additional constraints in the optimization process. 3. Risk that perceptual improvements do not translate to significant practical benefits for specific applications.
